result:
I keep thinking about the way water remembers.

Not in some poetic, metaphorical sense—though there’s plenty of that too—but in the actual, physical way hydrogen bonds rearrange themselves around anything they touch. A fingerprint dissolves, but the pattern of it lingers in the shifted lattice of molecules. Nothing is ever truly erased; it’s just translated into a language we haven’t learned to read.

This is why I can’t throw away the cracked teacup. The fracture runs from the rim like lightning frozen mid-flash, and when the light hits it at 3:17 p.m.—I’ve checked—it casts a shadow that looks exactly like the coast of Iceland. My grandmother’s lips touched that rim thousands of times, sipping Earl Grey while she told me stories about the war that her own mother told her, stories that grow more delicate each year like frost on a windowpane. The cup holds them all, water remembering the heat of her breath, the slight tremor in her hand the winter before she died.

We think memory lives in the brain, in those soft folds of gray matter that somehow convert electricity into grief, into the smell of cinnamon when you were seven, into the exact sound of your father starting the car on mornings when the world was still dark and possible. But I’ve started to suspect it’s more democratic than that. Memory leaks. It pools in the copper pipes of old houses, hums in the transformers that squat on telephone poles like mechanical Buddhas, gets carried by starlings who steal bright things for reasons they’ve forgotten.

Yesterday I found my neighbor’s diary in the recycling bin. She’d moved overnight—just gone, leaving the mattress and her grandmother’s mirror and this small blue book that shouldn’t have been thrown away. I didn’t read it. Instead I left it in the alley where the rain could get to it, where the ink could run and bleed into the asphalt, becoming something else entirely. A map. A spell. A stain that looks like Iceland.

We are all just water trying to remember what it was like to be glaciers.
